examples.title: Store Word (SW) Operation Sequence
examples.description: Pseudocode for executing a store word instruction showing register transfers and timing
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the three-cycle store operation. Cycle 1 computes and loads the effective address into MAR. Cycle 2 loads the data from the source register into MDR. Cycle 3 asserts the write signal and waits for MFC before actually transferring data. The WAIT UNTIL loop simulates stalling the processor until memory completes the operation.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - uses fixed set of registers

examples.title: Store Buffer with Forwarding
examples.description: Pseudocode showing store buffer operation and store-to-load forwarding in a pipelined processor
examples.code:
// Store Buffer Structure
STRUCT StoreBufferEntry
 VALID: boolean
 ADDRESS: word
 DATA: word
END_STRUCT

// Global store buffer
storeBuffer: ARRAY[0..3] OF StoreBufferEntry
storeBufferHead: integer ← 0
storeBufferTail: integer ← 0

// Function to handle store instruction
PROCEDURE ExecuteStore(regData, address)
BEGIN
 // Add entry to store buffer (fast commit)
 storeBuffer[storeBufferTail].VALID ← TRUE
 storeBuffer[storeBufferTail].ADDRESS ← address
 storeBuffer[storeBufferTail].DATA ← regData
 storeBufferTail ← (storeBufferTail + 1) MOD 4
 
 // Notify pipeline to continue (no stall)
END

// Function to handle load with store-buffer forwarding
PROCEDURE ExecuteLoad(regDest, address)
BEGIN
 // Check store buffer for matching address (forwarding)
 FOR i ← storeBufferHead TO storeBufferTail-1
 IF storeBuffer[i].VALID AND storeBuffer[i].ADDRESS = address THEN
 regDest ← storeBuffer[i].DATA // Forward from buffer
 RETURN
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // If not in buffer, access cache/memory normally
 regDest ← M[address]
END
examples.explanation: The store buffer allows fast commit of store instructions without waiting for memory. When executing a store, entry is added to buffer and pipeline continues. When executing a load, the buffer is checked first - if data is found, it's forwarded immediately without accessing memory. This eliminates most store-load stalls in pipelined processors.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for store commit, O(n) for buffer check where n=buffer size

examples.title: Cache Write Operation (Write-Through vs Write-Back)
examples.description: Pseudocode comparing write-through and write-back cache policies during a store hit
examples.code:
// Cache line structure
STRUCT CacheLine
 VALID: boolean
 DIRTY: boolean // Modified bit for write-back
 TAG: word
 DATA: array[0..15] of byte
END_STRUCT

// Write-through cache write
PROCEDURE WriteThrough(address, data)
BEGIN
 // Find cache line (assumes hit)
 cacheLine ← GetCacheLine(address)
 
 // Write to cache
 cacheLine.DATA[offset] ← data
 
 // Write to main memory (immediately)
 M[address] ← data
 
 // Write-through: both updated
END

// Write-back cache write
PROCEDURE WriteBack(address, data)
BEGIN
 cacheLine ← GetCacheLine(address)
 
 // If line is dirty, write back to memory first
 IF cacheLine.DIRTY THEN
 WriteBackToMemory(cacheLine.TAG, cacheLine.DATA)
 END IF
 
 // Write to cache only
 cacheLine.DATA[offset] ← data
 cacheLine.DIRTY ← TRUE
 
 // Main memory updated on eviction (later)
END

// Eviction handler
PROCEDURE EvictCacheLine(cacheLine)
BEGIN
 IF cacheLine.DIRTY THEN
 // Write back modified data
 WriteBackToMemory(cacheLine.TAG, cacheLine.DATA)
 END IF
 cacheLine.VALID ← FALSE
 cacheLine.DIRTY ← FALSE
END
examples.explanation: Write-through writes to both cache and memory on every store, ensuring consistency but slower performance. Write-back writes only to cache, marking the line dirty. The modified data is written to memory only when that cache line is evicted to make room for new data. Write-back is faster for multiple writes to the same location.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) cache write; O(1) for write-back hit, O(memory latency) for write-through
